records.ety: [OE. las, OF. laz, F. lacs, dim. lacet, fr. L. laqueus noose, snare; prob. akin to lacere to entice. Cf. Delight, Elicit, Lasso, Latchet.]

defs: That which binds or holds, especially by being interwoven; a string, cord, or band, usually one passing through eyelet or other holes, and used in drawing and holding together parts of a garment, of a shoe, of a machine belt, etc.
quotes: His hat hung at his back down by a lace.
For striving more, the more in laces strong Himself he tied.

defs: A snare or gin, especially one made of interwoven cords; a net.
quotes: Vulcanus had caught thee [Venus] in his lace.

defs: A fabric of fine threads of linen, silk, cotton, etc., often ornamented with figures; a delicate tissue of thread, much worn as an ornament of dress.
quotes: Our English dames are much given to the wearing of costly laces.

defs: Spirits added to coffee or some other beverage.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Laced (āst); p. pr. & vb. n. Lacing (?).]

defs: To fasten with a lace; to draw together with a lace passed through eyelet holes; to unite with a lace or laces, or, figuratively. with anything resembling laces.
quotes: When Jenny's stays are newly laced.

defs: To adorn with narrow strips or braids of some decorative material; as, cloth laced with silver.
To beat; to lash; to make stripes on.
quotes: I'll lace your coat for ye.

defs: To add something to (a food or beverage) so as to impart flavor, pungency, or some special quality; as, to lace a punch with alcohol; to lace the Kool-Aid with LSD.
To twine or draw as a lace; to interlace; to intertwine.
quotes: The Gond . . . picked up a trail of the Karela, the vine that bears the bitter wild gourd, and laced it to and fro across the temple door.

defs: To be fastened with a lace, or laces; as, these boots lace.
